remaining_tasks.name: On-device Debug verification — Sparkle pipeline + badge
remaining_tasks.progress: Build/crash/positioning bugs fixed and verified; badge tap-to-open-dialog still broken; several checklist steps unconfirmed (see blockers)

remaining_tasks.name: On-device Release verification — embedded Sparkle.framework launch

blockers.description: Tapping the update-available badge does not open Sparkle's dialog — no '[HUD-06] update badge tapped' console line appears on click, so the tap is not reaching the SwiftUI gesture handler at all. Suspected cause: NotchWindowController.handlePointer's click-through hotZone is sized to the narrow idle-pill geometry only (activeZone = hotZone when !isExpanded, computed once from collapsedFrame) and does not widen for the currently-active Charging/Device/Focus/OSD wing state, which is up to ~433pt wide vs the pill's ~179pt — clicks near the badge's position during a wide wing state would fall outside hotZone and pass through to the desktop. NOT yet confirmed in the plain idle state specifically (no wing active). One report ('opens an activity-center-like overview') is more consistent with the click falling through to collapsedIsland's own onClick() (expand) than a true click-through-to-desktop miss, so the exact mechanism is still ambiguous.
blockers.workaround: Use Xcode's Debug View Hierarchy (paused mid-attempt this session, capture failed once — retry when app is responsive) to get the badge's actual on-screen hit-testable frame and compare against hotZone bounds.

blockers.description: Several 40-03 Task 1 checklist steps were never explicitly confirmed amid the debugging detour: (2) quit+relaunch-twice shows no unprompted Sparkle permission alert (Pitfall 1 regression), (6) badge color follows Now Playing accent live, (7/8) badge disappears on expand / reappears on collapse, (10) hover→expand→move-down click-through trace shows no regression, (11) Settings 'Automatically Check for Updates' toggle OFF/ON persists correctly via defaults read.
blockers.workaround: Re-run these specific steps once the badge-tap bug is resolved — don't re-do the whole 13-step list, just the ones not explicitly confirmed.

human_actions_pending.action: Resume on-device debugging of the badge-tap bug using Xcode's Debug View Hierarchy tool
human_actions_pending.context: Remote screenshot-based debugging hit diminishing returns after 3 rounds of badge-positioning fixes and an unconfirmed tap-dispatch bug; need direct on-device geometry inspection.

decisions.rationale: The user's actual Xcode GUI session runs Xcode 27 beta (macOS 27.0 SDK); the default `xcode-select -p` points at /Applications/Xcode.app, a completely different toolchain. Several early 'passing' CLI verifications were meaningless because they exercised the wrong toolchain and never hit the real Xcode-beta-specific build bugs.

decisions.decision: Never delete ~/Library/Developer/Xcode/DerivedData/Islet-* while the user's Xcode GUI session may be open
decisions.rationale: Two separate races happened this session: a partial rm -rf left a corrupted half-deleted DerivedData tree, and a later wipe caused 'Missing package product' GUI build failures because SPM re-resolution didn't complete before the user hit Cmd+R. Always re-run `xcodebuild -resolvePackageDependencies` (with the correct DEVELOPER_DIR) immediately after any DerivedData wipe, before asking the user to rebuild.

decisions.decision: Sparkle's SUFeedURL cannot use file:// for local testing — URLSession's dataTask does not fetch file:// URLs (fails with a generic 'error retrieving update information', no useful detail surfaced to the user). Use a local HTTP server (python3 -m http.server bound to 127.0.0.1) instead; loopback HTTP doesn't need an ATS exception.
decisions.rationale: Confirmed by reproducing: file:// SUFeedURL failed identically across multiple rebuild attempts; switching to http://127.0.0.1:PORT/appcast-mock.xml immediately fetched and displayed the mock update correctly.

decisions.decision: The update-available badge overlay must track whichever collapsed-ish presentation (idle pill / Charging / Device / Focus / OSD wings) is actually on screen, not a fixed alignment on the outer expanded-sized container
decisions.rationale: D-05 (LOCKED, 40-CONTEXT.md) requires the badge to 'stay visible regardless of whatever else is currently showing there'. A GeometryReader+PreferenceKey+overlayPreferenceValue+named-coordinate-space attempt did NOT work on-device (badge rendered stuck near the container origin regardless of case — preference never propagated a real value). The working fix uses @State + onChange(of: proxy.size.width) to track just the active shape's WIDTH, then .overlay(alignment: .top) with an HStack sized to that width — avoids named-coordinate-space machinery entirely since .top alignment already matches presentationSwitch's own vertical position for free.

next_action: Relaunch Islet in Xcode, use Debug View Hierarchy (Xcode debug bar, 3-stacked-squares icon) while the app is responsive to inspect the badge's actual rendered frame and hit-test region vs NotchWindowController's hotZone, then fix the click-through/gesture-dispatch gap so the badge tap opens Sparkle's dialog. After that, re-confirm the remaining unconfirmed checklist steps (see blockers), then proceed to 40-03 Task 2 (Release archive verification).
context_notes: Phase 40 code (Sparkle backend, badge rendering) is functionally correct and merged (40-01/40-02). This session's entire time went into 40-03's on-device Debug checkpoint, which surfaced FOUR real bugs beyond what the plan anticipated: an AppDelegate notification-loop crash, a second Xcode-GUI-specific Sparkle SPM embed toolchain bug, Sparkle's file:// URL limitation, and three rounds of badge-positioning fixes before landing on a working approach. All of those are fixed and committed (b80f35d). The ONE open item is badge-tap dispatch — genuinely unresolved, not just unverified. The user was worn down by a long debugging session (many rounds, timestamps into the early-morning hours) and chose to pause rather than keep going blind without Xcode's View Hierarchy tool. Do not re-attempt more screenshot-based guessing on the tap bug — go straight to View Hierarchy inspection next time.
